attributes.stem: A 65-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of progressively increasing widespread lesions on his hands, feet, extremities and trunk for the past four months. According to him, the lesions are associated with mild itching but no fever or pain. He works in a chemical factory and has recently travelled from Africa five months ago. His medical history includes chronic urticaria for which he is taking corticosteroids daily, and he is a person who injects drugs recreationally. On examination, there are multiple, hyperkeratotic, crusted plaques over his trunk and extremities. Burrows are seen on the interdigital web spaces of his hands and feet. Microscopic examination of the scrapings of lesions demonstrates mites and their eggs.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely risk factor for the development of this condition?
options: Recent travel to a tropical region
attributes.explanation:
The patient in this scenario has presented with signs and symptoms correlating with crusted scabies, which is a variant of scabies characterised by exceptionally high parasite load that leads to the formation of scales or hyperkeratotic plaques. The diagnosis of crusted scabies is further confirmed by the presence of burrows in interdigital web spaces and by the demonstration of mites and their eggs on skin-scraping samples. The patient with crusted scabies usually presents with mild or no itching complaints. The most likely risk factor for the development of crusted scabies in this patient is corticosteroid treatment, which has led to a state of immunosuppression. Immunosuppression is one of the widely recognised risk factors for the development of crusted scabies. Other immunosuppressive conditions such as human immunodeficiency virus infection, organ transplantation, and use of immunosuppressive medications can also impair the immune response, leading to severe and extensive forms of scabies.

Advanced age is not a specific risk factor for crusted scabies. Although scabies infestations may occur more frequently in older adults due to factors such as reduced immune function and a higher likelihood of living in institutional settings, the development of crusted scabies is not solely attributed to age.

A person who injects drugs is at risk of other skin conditions such as cellulitis and other bacterial infections, but it is not an important risk factor for the development of crusted scabies.

Occupational exposure to chemicals can cause other skin conditions such as eczema, but it is not implicated in the development of crusted scabies.

Although scabies infestations can be more common in certain regions, especially in crowded and resource-limited areas, recent travel to a tropical region is not the most likely risk factor for crusted scabies in this patient. The characteristic clinical findings and history suggest a different primary risk factor.

Scabies is transmitted person-to-person through close skin contact,. Fomite transmission (via clothing or bedding) is uncommon in classic scabies but may be important in crusted scabies due to the high mite burden. Asymptomatic transmission can occur before symptoms develop.
learningPoints: Immunosuppression, particularly from corticosteroid use, is a key risk factor for crusted scabies.
Crusted scabies presents with hyperkeratotic, crusted plaques and may have minimal pruritus despite extensive infestation.
Scabies is primarily transmitted through close skin-to-skin contact, with crusted scabies posing a higher risk of transmission, including via contaminated items.
references: Cohen PR. Classic and Non-classic (Surrepticius) Scabies: Diagnostic and Treatment Considerations. Cureus. 2020 Mar 25;12(3):e7419. doi: 10.7759/cureus.7419. Erratum in: Cureus. 2020 May 2;12(5):c30. PMID: 32351804; PMCID: PMC7186094.
World Health Organization. Scabies [Internet]. World Health Organization. 2023. Available from: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/scabies
